content:
I've got a rather special problem. I have described it in detail here.

Basically:
I have an Opaque<T>, where that Opaque gets dropped at some point. But the memory backing both Opaque and T itself remains valid. And the T within remains being accessible through different parties (the memory is kept alive through refcounting).

Am I guaranteed that the Rust compiler will not invent any writes to that Opaque?

I have read through the documentations of Opaque, UnsafeCell and MaybeUninit and none of them seem to make any guarantee regarding what happens after drop().
What kind of writes are you concerned about? Compiler does not invent out of thin air writes. Drop will not run for T because of MaybeUninit.
I need the guarantee that no byte will be changed for my T by anyone except for myself through my well defined API functions, even if I do unsafe { drop_in_place(struct_that_contains_that_opaque) };
@Gary Guo told me that, hypothetically, the compiler can write into the memory of dropped data, if I understood him correctly.
If the Opaque is on the stack, sure that will be a problem. But if it is heap allcoated and not reclaimed, I don't see how the compiler would reuse the space without getting it from an allocator.
Bytes under Opaque are truly opaque and they are not considered to be dereferenceable by LLVM

Philipp Stanner said:

Gary Guo told me that, hypothetically, the compiler can write into the memory of dropped data, if I understood him correctly.

The compiler can do it during the drop, just like any function that takes mutable references (and the bytes not covered by UnsafePinned (or Opaque)
Typo? You mean "Bytes under Opaque are truly opaque"?
BTW, why are you dropping the DriverFenceData, rather than just dropping the data fields?

OK ^^
So the only way through which LLVM can change / touch those bytes is through opaque.get(), ack?

Gary Guo said:

BTW, why are you dropping the DriverFenceData, rather than just dropping the data fields?

Why does it matter? When you drop a struct, you typically just drop the entire struct.

I suppose you mean dropping the fields is a good idea to make "this does not drop" very explicit?

Or when the entire allocation is gone, but that is observable by LLVM level for strack variables as Andreas pointed out (technically free functions too, but LLVM only recognizes specific ones for such and not the kfree).

Philipp Stanner said:

Why does it matter? When you drop a struct, you typically just drop the entire struct.

I suppose you mean dropping the fields is a good idea to make "this does not drop" very explicit?

Yes, because that part of the struct stays for longer. I think it's probably not the best for the DriverFenceData::drop to be only sound because Fence is Opaque<...>, that reasoning chain is a bit convoluted so is better avoided

Yeah, good idea, that sounds fare more robust. And it should eliminate the problem even in the hypothetical.
Will do :+1:
Philipp Stanner has marked this topic as resolved.
